topics.title: JVM / JRE / JDK Architecture
topics.description: Understand the three-layer Java platform: JDK (developer tools + JRE), JRE (runtime libraries + JVM), and JVM (bytecode executor). Learn how Java achieves platform independence through bytecode and the class-loading mechanism.
topics.story: Imagine you're a novelist who wants your book read in every country. Instead of translating your manuscript into 50 languages yourself, you write it in a universal 'script language'. Each country has a local translator (JVM) who reads that script and converts it live for local readers. The JDK is your full writing studio — manuscript + editing tools. The JRE is the reader's bookshelf — the translator and standard reference books. The JVM is the translator performing live. Your manuscript (bytecode) travels the world unchanged.
topics.analogy: JDK = a chef's full kitchen (knives, oven, ingredients, recipes). JRE = the restaurant dining room + kitchen appliances (just enough to serve food). JVM = the head chef who takes a recipe card (bytecode) and cooks it for that specific stove model. Same recipe card works on a gas stove in Tokyo or electric in Paris — the chef adapts.
topics.memoryAnchor: JDK contains JRE contains JVM — like Russian nesting dolls. D=Developer (outermost, biggest), R=Runtime (middle), V=Virtual Machine (innermost, smallest). DRV: 'Developers Run Virtual machines'.
topics.firstPrinciples: Problem: OS-specific machine code makes software non-portable. Solution: introduce an intermediate representation (bytecode) that is OS-agnostic, then create OS-specific translators (JVMs). The JVM is a contract: any OS that implements the JVM spec can run any Java bytecode. JDK and JRE are just packaging decisions around this core insight.
topics.bruteForce: Without JVM: compile C++ to Windows x64 machine code. To run on Linux, recompile. For Mac, recompile again. Maintenance nightmare: three codebases, three build pipelines, three sets of OS bugs to handle.
topics.optimizedApproach: Java's two-phase compilation: javac → .class (bytecode, OS-agnostic) → JVM interprets/JIT-compiles to native code at runtime. One source → one bytecode → runs on any JVM implementation. JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler inside JVM further optimizes hot code paths to native speed.
topics.whenToUse: Understanding JVM internals matters when: diagnosing OutOfMemoryError (heap vs metaspace), tuning GC flags (-Xmx, -Xms), profiling with JVisualVM, understanding classloader conflicts (different JAR versions), analyzing why reflection is slow (bypasses JIT optimizations), or building frameworks that manipulate bytecode (Spring AOP, Mockito).

examples.title: Bytecode Inspection — javap Output
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"int x = 10;","variables":{"x":10},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"bipush 10 + istore_1: JVM pushes 10 onto operand stack, then pops into local var slot 1"},{"line":4,"lineCode":"int y = 20;","variables":{"x":10,"y":20},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"bipush 20 + istore_2: same pattern for y in slot 2"},{"line":5,"lineCode":"int sum = x + y;","variables":{"x":10,"y":20,"sum":30},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"iload_1 + iload_2 pushes both onto stack; iadd pops both and pushes 30; istore_3 saves to sum"},{"line":6,"lineCode":"System.out.println(\"Sum: \" + sum);","variables":{"x":10,"y":20,"sum":30},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"getstatic loads System.out reference; string concat creates new String; invokevirtual dispatches println"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[.java Source File] -->|javac compiler| B[.class Bytecode File]
    B -->|ClassLoader| C[JVM Method Area]
    C -->|Interpreter / JIT| D[Native Machine Code]
    D --> E[CPU Executes]
    
    subgraph JVM Internals
      C
      F[Heap - Objects]
      G[Stack - Frames]
      H[PC Register]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
SOURCE (.java)
  → javac → BYTECODE (.class)
    → ClassLoader loads into Method Area
      → Interpreter executes bytecodes one-by-one
        OR JIT compiles hot methods to native code
          → CPU runs native instructions
examples.explanation: javap -c reveals the JVM instruction set operating on a stack machine. Every operation pushes/pops the operand stack. Local variables live in numbered slots. This bytecode is identical on Windows, Linux, and Mac — only the JVM implementation differs per OS.
examples.realWorldUse: Framework authors (Spring, Hibernate) use bytecode manipulation libraries (ASM, Javassist) to inject behavior at load time — the same bytecode format that javap shows. Profilers like YourKit instrument bytecode to measure method call counts without changing source code.

examples.title: ClassLoader Hierarchy Demo
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"ClassLoader appLoader = ClassLoaderDemo.class.getClassLoader();","variables":{"appLoader":"AppClassLoader"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"getClassLoader() returns the loader that loaded this .class file — typically the Application ClassLoader"},{"line":5,"lineCode":"ClassLoader extLoader = appLoader.getParent();","variables":{"appLoader":"AppClassLoader","extLoader":"PlatformClassLoader"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Parent of AppCL is PlatformClassLoader (formerly ExtClassLoader in Java 8)"},{"line":6,"lineCode":"ClassLoader bootLoader = extLoader.getParent();","variables":{"appLoader":"AppClassLoader","extLoader":"PlatformClassLoader","bootLoader":"null"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Bootstrap ClassLoader is written in native C — represented as null in Java"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"String.class.getClassLoader()","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Returns null — String is a core class loaded by Bootstrap CL which has no Java representation"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph BT A[AppClassLoader
classpath, user JARs] -->|parent| B[PlatformClassLoader
JDK modules: java.sql, java.xml] B -->|parent| C[Bootstrap ClassLoader
java.lang, java.util, rt.jar] D[New class load request] --> A A -->|delegates UP first| B B -->|delegates UP first| C C -->|not found, back down| B B -->|not found, back down| A A -->|loads from classpath| E[Class in memory]
examples.pseudocode:
LOAD(className):
  delegate to parent first
  if parent found it → return
  else load from own classpath
  if not found → ClassNotFoundException
examples.explanation: Parent delegation prevents classpath pollution: user code cannot accidentally replace java.lang.String by placing a String.class on the classpath — Bootstrap always wins for core classes. Custom ClassLoaders (used by OSGi, Tomcat, Spring Boot fat JARs) override this to enable plugin isolation.
examples.realWorldUse: Tomcat runs multiple web apps in one JVM by giving each webapp its own ClassLoader with a private classpath — so webapp-A's log4j-1.x and webapp-B's log4j-2.x coexist without conflict. Same trick used by Spring Boot's fat JAR loader for nested JARs.

examples.title: JVM Memory Areas — Stack vs Heap
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":3,"lineCode":"static int counter = 0;","variables":{"counter":0},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Static field stored in Method Area (Metaspace in Java 8+), shared across all instances"},{"line":6,"lineCode":"int localVar = 42;","variables":{"localVar":42},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Primitive int stored directly in main()'s stack frame — no heap allocation needed"},{"line":7,"lineCode":"String msg = \"hello\";","variables":{"msg":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"'msg' variable (reference) on stack; actual String object on heap. String literals go to String Pool"},{"line":8,"lineCode":"MemoryDemo obj = new MemoryDemo();","variables":{"localVar":42,"msg":"ref→Heap@0x100","obj":"ref→Heap@0x200"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"new keyword allocates MemoryDemo object on heap; 'obj' reference lives on stack"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"static String greet(String name) {","variables":{"name":"ref→Heap@0x100"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"New stack frame pushed for greet(). 'name' is a copy of the reference — both point to same heap object"},{"line":14,"lineCode":"String result = \"Hi \" + name;","variables":{"name":"ref→Heap@0x100","result":"ref→Heap@0x300"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"String concatenation creates new String object on heap at 0x300"},{"line":15,"lineCode":"return result;","variables":{},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"Stack frame for greet() popped. Returned reference (0x300) copied to caller's frame"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    subgraph Stack
      F2["Frame: greet()\nname → 0x100\nresult → 0x300"]
      F1["Frame: main()\nlocalVar = 42\nmsg → 0x100\nobj → 0x200"]
    end
    subgraph Heap
      H1["0x100: String 'hello'"]
      H2["0x200: MemoryDemo obj"]
      H3["0x300: String 'Hi hello'"]
    end
    subgraph MethodArea
      M1["MemoryDemo.class\ncounter = 0"]
    end
    F2 --> H1
    F2 --> H3
    F1 --> H1
    F1 --> H2
examples.pseudocode:
STACK: one frame per method call, LIFO, stores primitives + references
HEAP: all objects (new ...), shared across threads, GC-managed
METHOD AREA: class metadata, static fields, constant pool
PC REGISTER: per-thread pointer to current bytecode instruction
examples.explanation: This is the most critical JVM concept for debugging. StackOverflowError = too many nested method calls (frames overflow). OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space = heap full (too many objects). OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace = too many classes loaded (Method Area full). Understanding this maps every production error to a root cause.
examples.realWorldUse: JVM flags: -Xss controls stack size (default 512KB-1MB per thread), -Xmx controls max heap size, -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize controls Method Area. In microservices with 1000 concurrent threads, -Xss256k vs default can save 256MB of RAM per service instance.

examples.title: JIT Compilation — Warm-Up Effect
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":11,"lineCode":"for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++)","variables":{"i":0,"phase":"cold"},"phase":"LOOP_START","annotation":"JVM starts in interpreter mode — each bytecode instruction decoded one at a time. Slow but no warm-up cost"},{"line":13,"lineCode":"compute(100_000)","variables":{"callCount":1},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Interpreter executes compute(). JVM's profiler counts invocations and notes this is a 'hot' method"},{"line":19,"lineCode":"for (int i = 0; i < 200; i++)","variables":{"i":0,"phase":"JIT"},"phase":"LOOP_START","annotation":"After ~10k calls, C1 JIT compiler kicks in. Compiles compute() to optimized native machine code"},{"line":21,"lineCode":"compute(100_000)","variables":{"callCount":10000},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Now calling native code directly — no bytecode decoding. C2 compiler may further optimize with inlining, loop unrolling"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph LR
    A[Bytecode] --> B{Hot?}
    B -->|No, < 1000 calls| C[Interpreter\nSlow but immediate]
    B -->|Yes, > 1000 calls| D[C1 JIT Compiler\nFast compilation\nBasic optimizations]
    D --> E{Very hot?}
    E -->|> 10000 calls| F[C2 JIT Compiler\nSlow compilation\nAggressive optimizations\ninlining, unrolling]
    E -->|No| G[C1 Native Code]
    F --> H[C2 Native Code\n~100x faster than interpreter]
examples.pseudocode:
Tier 0: Interpreter (immediate startup)
Tier 1: C1 with no profiling (simple methods)
Tier 2: C1 with limited profiling
Tier 3: C1 with full profiling
Tier 4: C2 full optimization (hot methods)
examples.explanation: Java appears slow on startup because JIT hasn't warmed up yet. This is why benchmarks MUST include warm-up iterations (JMH framework forces 5+ warm-up rounds). In production, Spring Boot apps seem 'slow for first requests' — that's JIT warm-up. GraalVM Native Image pre-compiles everything to eliminate warm-up entirely.
examples.realWorldUse: Kubernetes rolling deploys send production traffic to new pods immediately. Cold JIT = slow initial requests = cascading timeouts. Solutions: (1) warm-up traffic via health check probes before marking pod as ready, (2) GraalVM Native Image (used by Quarkus/Micronaut), (3) JVM tiered compilation flags: -XX:+TieredCompilation (default in Java 8+).

examples.title: Garbage Collection — GC Roots and Reachability
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":4,"lineCode":"String alive = \"I'm reachable...\"","variables":{"alive":"ref→Heap"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"'alive' is a GC root — it lives in main()'s stack frame. Objects referenced from stack frames are always reachable"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"createGarbage();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"New stack frame for createGarbage(). All objects created inside are reachable via this frame"},{"line":19,"lineCode":"StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();","variables":{"sb":"ref→Heap@0xABC"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"sb allocated on heap. Referenced from createGarbage() frame — currently reachable"},{"line":22,"lineCode":"String big = sb.toString();","variables":{"sb":"ref→Heap@0xABC","big":"ref→Heap@0xDEF"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"big allocated on heap. Both sb and big reachable while createGarbage() executes"},{"line":23,"lineCode":"// big and sb go out of scope","variables":{},"phase":"RETURN","annotation":"createGarbage() frame popped. sb and big references gone. Heap objects 0xABC and 0xDEF now unreachable — GC eligible"},{"line":11,"lineCode":"System.gc();","variables":{"alive":"ref→Heap"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"GC traces from roots (stack refs + static refs). 'alive' and CACHED reachable. sb/big garbage collected"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    subgraph GC_Roots
      R1[Stack Frame: alive]
      R2[Static Field: CACHED]
    end
    subgraph Heap
      O1[alive String]
      O2[StringBuilder sb]
      O3[String big]
      O4[CACHED String]
    end
    R1 -->|references| O1
    R2 -->|references| O4
    O2 -.->|unreachable after return| X1[GC eligible]
    O3 -.->|unreachable after return| X1
examples.pseudocode:
GC MARK phase:
  start from GC roots (stack vars, statics, JNI refs)
  traverse all references recursively
  mark everything reachable
GC SWEEP phase:
  collect all unmarked objects on heap
  (or COMPACT: move live objects together)
examples.explanation: GC eligibility is purely about reachability from GC roots, not about scope or nulling variables. Calling obj = null removes one reference but if another reference to the same object exists anywhere (static field, collection, callback), the object stays alive. This is the source of most Java memory leaks — a long-lived collection holding references to objects you think you've 'deleted'.
examples.realWorldUse: Classic Java memory leak: static Map cache that grows unbounded. Objects added to the map are GC roots via the static field — they never get collected. Fix: use WeakHashMap (keys held weakly — collected when no other strong reference exists) or explicit cache eviction (Caffeine, Guava Cache with expiry).

problems.title: Identify Output — Stack vs Heap
problems.hint1: Primitive types (int, double, boolean, etc.) are stored directly on the stack when they are local variables.
problems.hint2: Objects created with 'new' keyword always go to the heap. String literals also go to the heap (String Pool area).
problems.hint3: Array types are objects — even int[] is an object on the heap. The variable 'arr' (the reference) is on the stack, but the array itself is on the heap.

testCases.output: {staticVal=method-area, a=stack, b=stack, s_ref=stack, s_obj=heap, arr_ref=stack, arr_obj=heap}

problems.constraints: No constraints — conceptual exercise. Focus on correct classification.
problems.editorial: Primitives (int, double, boolean) stored directly in the stack frame. Object references are on the stack, but the object itself is on the heap. Static fields live in the Method Area (Metaspace). int[] is an object — the array is on the heap, only the reference variable is on the stack.

problems.title: ClassLoader Chain Traversal
problems.description:
Write a method `printClassLoaderChain(Class<?> clazz)` that prints the full ClassLoader hierarchy for a given class, from the class's own loader up to the Bootstrap loader (null).

Example output for `String.class`:
```
Bootstrap ClassLoader (null)
```

Example output for a user class:
```
AppClassLoader -> PlatformClassLoader -> Bootstrap (null)
```
problems.hint1: Use clazz.getClassLoader() to get the loader. Then call .getParent() repeatedly.
problems.hint2: Bootstrap ClassLoader is represented as null in Java. Your loop should stop when the loader is null.
problems.hint3: Build the chain by collecting loader names, then print them joined with ' -> '.
problems.constraints: Handle the case where getClassLoader() returns null immediately (Bootstrap-loaded classes).
problems.editorial: Walk the chain with getParent() in a while loop. Null signals the Bootstrap ClassLoader (written in C — has no Java representation). Collect names using getClass().getName() or the literal 'Bootstrap'. Join with ' -> '.

problems.title: Count GC-Eligible Objects
problems.hint1: After process() returns, all local variables (a, b, c, d, list) go out of scope.
problems.hint2: Count each distinct object: the 'alpha' String, the 'beta' String, the 'delta' String, and the ArrayList itself.
problems.hint3: Even though 'list' held a reference to 'b' while inside the method, once 'list' itself is unreachable (method returned), everything it references is also unreachable.
problems.constraints: Consider only objects explicitly created with 'new' in the process() method.
problems.editorial: After process() returns, all local references (sb, a, b, c, list) are gone. Objects they pointed to have no remaining strong reference — they are GC-eligible. Static fields (KEEP) are GC roots and are never eligible. Count every heap object that has no remaining path from a GC root.

problems.title: JVM Version Detection
problems.description: Write a method `getJvmInfo()` that returns a formatted string with: (1) the Java version, (2) the JVM vendor, (3) the JVM name, and (4) whether the current runtime is a JRE-only or full JDK installation. Format: `'Java 17.0.2 | GraalVM CE | OpenJDK 64-Bit | JDK'`
problems.hint1: Java system properties contain all JVM metadata. Use System.getProperty(key).
problems.hint2: Key properties: 'java.version', 'java.vendor', 'java.vm.name'. For JDK vs JRE check if javac (compiler) is available.
problems.hint3: To check for JDK, try Class.forName('com.sun.tools.javac.Main') or check if 'java.home' contains 'jdk' substring.

testCases.output: contains '|' separators and Java version number

problems.constraints: Must use System.getProperty() API only. Output format: 'version | vendor | vmName | JDK/JRE'.
problems.editorial: System.getProperty() reads JVM system properties at runtime. 'java.version', 'java.vendor', 'java.home' are always set. To detect JDK vs JRE, check for the presence of 'tools.jar' or try Class.forName('com.sun.tools.javac.Main').

problems.title: Object Reference Equality vs Value Equality
problems.description: Write a method `compareStrings()` that demonstrates the difference between == (reference equality) and .equals() (value equality) for Strings, explaining why `new String("abc") == new String("abc")` is false but `"abc" == "abc"` is true.
problems.hint1: String literals are interned into the String Pool on the heap. Same literal text → same object reference.
problems.hint2: new String(...) always creates a new object on the heap, bypassing the String Pool.
problems.hint3: Use == to compare references, .equals() to compare content. Return a boolean array [literal==, new==, equals].
problems.constraints: Demonstrate all three cases. Do not use String.intern().
problems.editorial: String literals with the same value are interned — 'abc' and 'abc' are the same object in the String Pool, so == is true. new String('abc') creates a distinct heap object — == is false. .equals() compares content and returns true for all three. This is the most common Java equality trap.

problems.title: StackOverflowError — Recursion Depth Limit
problems.description: Write a method `maxRecursionDepth()` that determines the maximum recursion depth before a StackOverflowError occurs on the current JVM. The method should catch the error and return the depth at which it occurred.
problems.hint1: Use a recursive method that increments a counter and passes it recursively.
problems.hint2: Wrap the recursive call in try-catch(StackOverflowError e) — you can catch Errors in Java.
problems.hint3: The depth varies by JVM stack size (-Xss flag) and frame size. Typical values: 5,000-20,000 depending on JVM settings.
problems.constraints: Must catch StackOverflowError (not Exception). Return value should be > 1000 on any standard JVM.
problems.editorial: Catch StackOverflowError (extends Error, not Exception) and return the depth counter. Frame size depends on local variables — add more locals to reduce max depth. Default JVM stack size is 512KB-1MB per thread; increase with -Xss. Typical values: 5,000–20,000 frames.

problems.title: Simulate Simple Class Loading Order
problems.hint1: Static fields and static blocks are executed in TOP-TO-BOTTOM textual order when the class is first accessed.
problems.hint2: A method call in a field initializer (like initA()) is executed at that position in the order.
problems.hint3: The static block runs at the position it appears in the file, not at the end.
problems.constraints: Do not reorder fields. Output must match the textual initialization order exactly.
problems.editorial: Static fields and static initializer blocks execute in textual (top-to-bottom) order when the class is first loaded. A static block reads/writes fields declared above it — fields below are not yet initialized. The JVM holds the class initialization lock, preventing concurrent initialization.

problems.title: WeakReference vs StrongReference
problems.description:
Demonstrate the difference between a strong reference and a WeakReference in terms of GC eligibility. Write a method that:
1. Creates an object with a strong reference — verify it survives GC
2. Creates an object with only a WeakReference — verify it is collected after GC hint

Return true if the WeakReference returns null after GC (object collected).
problems.hint1: java.lang.ref.WeakReference<T> holds a weak reference — GC can collect the referent even if the WeakReference object itself still exists.
problems.hint2: After creating the WeakReference, set the strong reference to null so only the weak reference remains.
problems.hint3: Call System.gc() as a hint and check weakRef.get() — returns null if the object was collected.
problems.constraints: Use java.lang.ref.WeakReference. Note: System.gc() is not guaranteed — test may occasionally return false.
problems.editorial: WeakReference allows GC to collect the referent when no strong reference exists. After nulling the strong reference and calling System.gc(), weakRef.get() returns null — the object was collected. Strong references always prevent GC regardless of gc() hints.

problems.title: JVM Flag Impact — Measure Heap Usage
problems.description: Write a method `measureHeapUsage()` that allocates 10,000 String objects, then measures and returns the approximate heap memory used (in bytes) using Runtime.getRuntime(). The method should print heap usage before and after allocation.
problems.hint1: Runtime.getRuntime().totalMemory() - Runtime.getRuntime().freeMemory() gives used heap bytes.
problems.hint2: Call System.gc() before measuring baseline to get a clean starting point.
problems.hint3: Store objects in a List to prevent GC from collecting them before you measure.
problems.constraints: Heap delta should be > 100,000 bytes for 10,000 String allocations.
problems.editorial: Runtime.getRuntime().totalMemory() - freeMemory() gives approximate used heap. Call gc() before measuring for a cleaner baseline. Retain references in a List to prevent premature GC. The difference between before and after allocation approximates the heap cost of those objects.

problems.title: Custom ClassLoader — Load Class from Bytes
problems.description:
Implement a `ByteArrayClassLoader` that extends ClassLoader and can load a class from a provided byte array (pre-compiled bytecode). The loader should:
1. Accept class name and byte[] in constructor
2. Override findClass() to call defineClass() with the byte array
3. Return an instance of the loaded class
problems.hint1: Extend ClassLoader and override findClass(String name).
problems.hint2: Inside findClass(), call this.defineClass(name, bytes, 0, bytes.length) to create a Class object from raw bytes.
problems.hint3: To test, compile a simple class to bytes using JavaCompiler API or use a pre-compiled byte array.

testCases.output: Loaded class name matches provided name

problems.constraints: Must extend ClassLoader and override findClass(). Must call defineClass() with the byte array.
problems.editorial: Override findClass(String name) and call defineClass(name, bytes, 0, bytes.length). The parent-delegation model means loadClass() delegates to parent first — findClass() is only called when parent fails. defineClass() registers the Class object in this loader's namespace.

problems.title: Metaspace OOM — Dynamic Class Generation
problems.description:
Demonstrate (in a controlled way) how loading too many classes can exhaust Metaspace. Write a method that dynamically generates and loads simple classes in a loop, counting how many can be loaded before OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace. Use a small Metaspace limit (-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=10m) for testing.

Note: This is a diagnostic exercise — return the count when OOM occurs.
problems.hint1: Use javax.tools.JavaCompiler or ASM library to generate class bytecode dynamically in a loop.
problems.hint2: Catch OutOfMemoryError and return the count. Each loaded class is stored in Metaspace.
problems.hint3: To prevent parent delegation from caching, create a new ClassLoader instance for each class.
problems.constraints: Conceptual exercise. Run with -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=10m in real test. Must catch OutOfMemoryError.
problems.editorial: Each ClassLoader instance maintains its own namespace. Create a fresh ByteClassLoader per class to bypass caching — otherwise loadClass() returns the same cached class. Track classes in a List to prevent GC. Metaspace fills when the list grows, as each Class object consumes Metaspace.

problems.title: Thread-Local Storage Demo
problems.description:
Implement a `RequestContext` class using ThreadLocal to store a per-thread request ID. Write:
1. A static ThreadLocal<String> field for requestId
2. `setRequestId(String id)` and `getRequestId()` methods
3. A demo showing two threads with different requestIds that don't interfere with each other
problems.hint1: Use ThreadLocal<String> as a static field. Each thread gets its own isolated copy.
problems.hint2: ThreadLocal.set() stores a value for the current thread. ThreadLocal.get() retrieves the current thread's value.
problems.hint3: Use CountDownLatch to synchronize thread completion in the demo.
problems.constraints: Must use ThreadLocal. Must call remove() after use to prevent memory leaks in thread pools.
problems.editorial: ThreadLocal.set() stores value in the current thread's internal map (Thread.threadLocals). remove() clears it — essential to prevent memory leaks in thread-pool environments (Tomcat, Spring) where threads are reused. Each thread sees its own isolated value.

problems.title: JVM Shutdown Hook
problems.description:
Register a JVM shutdown hook that prints a cleanup message when the JVM exits normally (via System.exit() or program completion). Write a program that:
1. Registers a shutdown hook thread
2. Performs some work
3. Calls System.exit(0)
4. Verifies the hook runs before JVM terminates
problems.hint1: Use Runtime.getRuntime().addShutdownHook(Thread hook) to register a shutdown hook.
problems.hint2: The hook is a Thread object. When JVM shuts down, all registered hooks run concurrently.
problems.hint3: Hooks run on System.exit(), normal completion, SIGTERM signal. They do NOT run on SIGKILL (-9).
problems.constraints: Must use Runtime.getRuntime().addShutdownHook(). Hook must complete before JVM fully exits.
problems.editorial: Runtime.getRuntime().addShutdownHook(new Thread(...)) registers a hook that runs when the JVM shuts down normally. Hooks execute concurrently — add synchronization if needed. SIGKILL (-9) bypasses hooks. Good for closing DB connections, flushing buffers.

problems.title: JVM Flags Parser
problems.description: Write a method `getJvmFlags()` that reads and returns a list of JVM arguments active for the current process using the ManagementFactory API. Filter and return only flags that start with '-X' or '-XX'.
problems.hint1: Use ManagementFactory.getRuntimeMXBean().getInputArguments() to get all JVM arguments.
problems.hint2: Filter the list to include only arguments starting with '-X' (heap, stack flags) or '-XX' (advanced tuning flags).
problems.hint3: This is useful in diagnostic tools and health endpoints to report the JVM configuration.
problems.constraints: Must use ManagementFactory.getRuntimeMXBean(). Run with -Xmx256m -Xss512k to see flags in output.
problems.editorial: ManagementFactory.getRuntimeMXBean().getInputArguments() returns JVM arguments passed at startup (-Xmx, -XX:+UseG1GC, etc.). Useful for diagnostics endpoints and health checks. Note: some JVM flags are not exposed this way — use -XX:+PrintFlagsFinal for the full list.

problems.title: Class Initialization Deadlock Detection
problems.description: Two classes A and B have static initializers that reference each other (circular dependency). Explain and demonstrate how the JVM handles this and what happens. Write a test that shows the behavior and explain why one class may see an uninitialized state of the other.
problems.hint1: The JVM uses a per-class lock during static initialization. If Thread-1 initializes A (which triggers B), and Thread-2 initializes B (which triggers A) simultaneously — deadlock.
problems.hint2: In single-threaded case with circular static init, the JVM detects the cycle: when A's init triggers B's init which tries to re-init A, A is in 'being initialized' state — JVM returns A's partially-initialized state to B.
problems.hint3: Result: B may see A's static fields at their default values (0, null, false) even though A's initializer hasn't completed yet.
problems.constraints: Circular static initialization is a REAL Java pitfall. Output must demonstrate the partial-initialization anomaly.
problems.editorial: Circular static initialization: A's static block references B, B's static block references A. The JVM class init lock prevents true deadlock, but A may observe B's fields at default values (0/null/false) before B's initializer runs. This is undefined initialization order — avoid circular static dependencies.

problems.title: GC-Safe Singleton with Lazy Initialization
problems.description:
Implement a thread-safe Singleton using the 'Initialization-on-Demand Holder' (IODH) idiom. This leverages the JVM's class loading guarantees to ensure thread safety WITHOUT explicit synchronization.

Also implement a comparison showing why double-checked locking (DCL) without volatile is broken on the JVM.
problems.hint1: IODH: put the singleton instance in a private static inner class. The inner class is loaded lazily (only when getInstance() is called) and class loading is inherently thread-safe.
problems.hint2: For DCL: without `volatile`, the JVM can reorder instructions. Instance might be published before constructor completes — other threads see non-null but partially constructed object.
problems.hint3: IODH has no synchronization overhead because class initialization locking (one-time cost) handles thread safety.
problems.constraints: IODH must use a static nested class as the holder. Fixed DCL must use volatile keyword.
problems.editorial: IODH leverages the JVM guarantee that class initialization is thread-safe and happens exactly once. The nested Holder class is not loaded until getInstance() is first called — giving lazy initialization without any synchronization overhead. Enum singleton is even simpler but less flexible.

problems.title: JVM Memory Leak Simulation and Fix
problems.description:
Write a program that demonstrates a classic Java memory leak (a growing static collection), measures heap growth over time, and then shows the fixed version using cache eviction.

The leaky version should show heap growing with each 'request'. The fixed version should cap memory usage.
problems.hint1: Classic leak: static Map<String, Object> cache that only adds, never removes. Objects added are GC roots via the static field.
problems.hint2: Measure heap before and after in a loop to demonstrate growth. Use Runtime.getRuntime().totalMemory() - freeMemory().
problems.hint3: Fix using LinkedHashMap with removeEldestEntry() override to cap size, or use a proper cache library pattern.
problems.constraints: Leaky version must show 10,000 entries. Fixed version must cap at MAX (1000) entries using removeEldestEntry.
problems.editorial: Static Map is a GC root — all values are permanently reachable and never collected. Fix: cap with LinkedHashMap(capacity, 0.75f, true) overriding removeEldestEntry. WeakHashMap keys are collected when no other strong reference exists — good when the key itself is the lifetime indicator.

problems.title: JVM Heap Dump Analysis Simulation
problems.description:
Write a diagnostic class `HeapAnalyzer` that simulates heap analysis by:
1. Creating 5 different object types with different counts and sizes
2. Using reflection to inspect objects at runtime
3. Reporting a 'top memory consumers' summary sorted by total bytes

This simulates what tools like Eclipse MAT or jmap do when analyzing heap dumps.
problems.hint1: Use a Map<String, Long> to track className -> totalBytes. Instrument allocations manually.
problems.hint2: For 'size' estimation, use Instrumentation interface (requires Java agent) or hardcode for demo.
problems.hint3: Sort the map by value descending to show top memory consumers.
problems.constraints: Must use Java record for ObjectStats. Must sort by totalBytes() descending. Must include 5+ object types.
problems.editorial: Track per-class counts and bytes-each. Sort by total bytes (count * bytesEach) descending. ObjectStats as a record gives free equals/hashCode/toString. The pattern mirrors jmap -histo output which shows live objects by class, count, and total bytes — critical for OOM diagnosis.

problems.title: JVM Tiered Compilation Benchmark
problems.description:
Write a proper JMH-style micro-benchmark (manual implementation without JMH dependency) that demonstrates JIT warm-up. The benchmark should:
1. Run a computation method 100,000 times
2. Track execution time in 10 batches of 10,000 calls
3. Show time decreasing as JIT optimizes the method
4. Report a summary showing cold vs warm performance
problems.hint1: Divide iterations into batches and measure each batch with System.nanoTime().
problems.hint2: Use a 'sink' variable (blackhole) to prevent JIT from eliminating dead code: if result is never used, JIT will optimize away the computation entirely.
problems.hint3: Expect batch 1-2 to be 10-100x slower than batch 8-10 due to JIT warm-up.
problems.constraints: Must use volatile blackhole sink. Must show at least 10 batches. Cold batch should be measurably slower than hot batch.
problems.editorial: Batch calls rather than measuring per-call to reduce System.nanoTime() overhead (each call ~25ns). Use a volatile blackhole field to prevent dead-code elimination. Expect 10-100x speedup after JIT warm-up. Report median not mean — JIT spikes make means misleading.

problems.title: Build a Minimal JVM Emulator
problems.description:
Implement a simple stack-based virtual machine that mimics how the JVM executes bytecode. Your emulator should support these instructions:
- PUSH n (push integer n onto stack)
- POP (pop and discard top of stack)
- ADD (pop two, push sum)
- MUL (pop two, push product)
- STORE var (pop and store in named variable)
- LOAD var (push variable value onto stack)
- PRINT (pop and print top of stack)

Execute a program that computes (3 + 4) * 2 and prints the result.
problems.hint1: Use a Deque<Integer> as the operand stack (LIFO). Use a Map<String,Integer> as the local variable table.
problems.hint2: Parse each instruction line: split by space, first token is opcode, optional second token is argument.
problems.hint3: PUSH pushes argument. ADD pops two values, pushes a+b. STORE pops and puts in map. LOAD reads from map and pushes.

testCases.input: PUSH 3, PUSH 4, ADD, PUSH 2, MUL, PRINT
PUSH 10, PUSH 5, ADD, STORE sum, LOAD sum, PUSH 3, MUL, PRINT

problems.constraints: Must support all 7 opcodes. Stack must be LIFO. Variables stored in HashMap. Must produce correct results for both test cases.
problems.editorial: Stack-based VM: PUSH puts value on deque, ADD pops two and pushes sum, STORE pops into variable map, LOAD pushes from map, PRINT pops and prints. This is exactly how the JVM operand stack works. Deque<Integer> as the stack with push/pop on same end gives LIFO semantics.
